original_text: Name: ___. Unit No: ___ Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___ Date of Birth: ___ Sex: F Service: SURGERY Allergies: Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs Attending: ___. Chief Complaint: SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: 1. Exploratory laparotomy. 2. Lysis of intraperitoneal adhesions History of Present Illness: This was a ___ woman, who in the remote past, had undergone a total abdominal hysterectomy for benign disease. ___ years prior to the present admission, she apparently had required hospitalization for a presumed small intestinal obstruction that had resolved without need for surgery. However, the patient presented to our emergency room 3 days prior to the present operation with a several-day history of abdominal discomfort, nausea and vomiting. A plain film of the abdomen followed by a CT scan suggested a distal fairly high-grade small bowel obstruction. She had essentially no gas in the colon and a convincing transition point in the very distal ileum with markedly dilated small intestine proximal to this. Her admission white blood cell count was elevated. She had mild right lower quadrant tenderness. I initially managed her with nasogastric decompression. However, her nasogastric tube was never very productive. She remained distended. On the third hospital day, her white blood count remained elevated at 14.6. She longer complained of discomfort. She actually had 1 or 2 small recorded bowel movements and reported passing flatus. However, she remained quite distended. A plain film the abdomen showed persistently dilated small intestine, measuring up to 5-6 cm. There was a paucity of gas within the colon. Although her situation was not completely straightforward, I found it disturbing that her white blood count remained elevated and that she had not made truly significant progress. Accordingly, I advised the patient and her family to undergo exploratory surgery to relieve her obstruction, and also to be certain that she did not have ischemic enteritis as a cause for her elevated white blood cell count. They freely consented to proceed. She was initially started on dexamethasone and posterior fossa mass mostly disappeared. She was monitored and it quickly returned along with symptoms, so had open biopsy in ___. Once lymphoma was confirmed she started high-dose methotrexate. After cycle 5, she had residual disease so continued on the Q2-week regimen. She presents today for admission for cycle #10 high dose methotrexate. Cycle #8 she had hand flushing/numbness after starting methotrexate, so infusion was stopped, but successfully finished the following day after premeds with Benadryl and dexamethasone. She had started having hives several days before that infusion. Cycle #9 she again had similar symptoms, infusion stopped after <20% of the dose. a few days later she had diffuse hives. She saw an allergist after discharge and IgE mediated methotrexate allergy was confirmed. On day of discharge, she reports feeling "tired", but "ready to roll" (home). She currently denies any problems, including hives or f/c. She reports the mild rash she has on her chest is chronic and non-pruritic. She reports typically having low grade temps with chemo. She endorses her usual mild nausea, which is controlled with prn ondansetron. No emesis, no cp, no sob, no diarrhea, no constipation, no abdominal pain and no dysuria. REVIEW OF SYSTEMS (on admit): (+) Per HPI (-) She denies fever, chills, night sweats, recent weight loss or gain. She denies headache, rhinorrhea or congestion. Denies cough, shortness of breath, or wheezing. She denies chest pain, chest pressure, or weakness. She denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain. She denies dysuria, arthralgias or myalgias. All other ROS negative Past Medical History: PAST ONCOLOGIC HISTORY: ___: She developed unsteady gait ___: Gadolinium-enhanced head MRI in ___ showing a right-sided posterior fossa mass. She was placed on dexamethasone at a dose of 4 mg every 6 hours. Her gait improved. ___: She established care with Dr. ___ in ___ with plan for resection of mass. HW preoperative MRI showed shrinkage of tumor to the point it nearly dissapeared. Surgery was cancelled and dexamethasone was stopped. ___: She developed worsening gait ataxia ___: A 2.3 cm x 2.8 cm enhancing mass centered in the right cerebellar hemisphere has increased in size since ___ examination, but is similar in appearance to ___ study. The lesion demonstrates restricted diffusion and central necrotic component. The lesion likely represents lymphoma, given its response to steroids. Additional differential considerations include metastatic disease or primary
